segments.s627-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” از کار رومی is a prepositional phrase: از = “from/of” and کار رومی = “the Roman/Byzantine affair/campaign” (کار here = affair). بپردخت is an older/poetic past form of پرداختن ‘to finish, settle, deal with’ (3rd singular), and شاه is the subject; the clause order is verb + subject in a classical/poetic style: “when… the king finished the Roman affair.”
segments.s627-g2: دلش = دل + enclitic possessive -ش (“his heart” / his feelings). گشت is past of گشتن used as a copular/ intransitive ‘became/turned.’ پیچان is a predicative adjective used poetically (‘twisting, restless, perplexed’). ز کار سپاه is a causal/prepositional phrase (ز = “from/because of” + کار سپاه = “the army’s affair[s]”); together: “his heart became troubled because of the army’s affairs.”
segments.s627-g3: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-form verb meaning “he commanded/said.” تا introduces a subordinate (purpose/result) clause ‘that/so that.’ موبد is the noun subject of the subordinate clause (a Zoroastrian priest) and رای‌زن is the predicate noun meaning “counselor/adviser.” The verb of the subordinate clause follows (see next segment).
segments.s627-g4: بشد is the subjunctive/optative 3rd-person form of شدن here introduced by تا: “that he be/may become.” با is the preposition “with.” یکی is an indefinite “a certain/one” (used here as ‘a certain person’). نامدار is an adjective “renowned,” and انجمن is the noun “assembly/council.” Together: “that the mobed be made counselor to sit in council with a certain renowned man.”
segments.s627-g5: ببخشید is the past verb of بخشیدن used here in the sense “he gave/bestowed.” روی زمین = “upon the face/surface of the earth” (روی = “on/upon,” زمین = “earth/ground”), and سربسر is an adverb meaning “all over / everywhere.” The verb precedes these adverbial/location phrases in the poetic word order: “He gave, all over the earth…”
segments.s627-g6: ابر here functions as a poetic/intensifying element prefixed to پهلوانان to indicate very great/chief heroes (a classical trope), followed by پهلوانان “champions, heroes.” پرخاشخر is an adjective/epithet meaning “quarrelsome / aggressive,” describing those champions. Word order: (intensifier) + noun (plural) + adjective.
segments.s627-g7: درم داد is the simple past construction ‘he gave dirhams’ (درم = dirham/coin, داد = gave). The following و + nouns (اسپ و نگین و کلاه) continue the list of items given: اسپ = horse/steed, نگین = gem, کلاه = hat. In poetic diction verbs and their objects can be arranged flexibly, so these items are all direct objects of داد.
segments.s627-g8: گرانمایه is an adjective ‘precious/valuable’ placed before the object-marking را for emphasis; را signals that what follows is the definite object(s) of the giving. کشور و تاج و گاه form a coordinated list: کشور = realm/country, تاج = crown (sovereignty), and گاه here denotes a seat/place (poetic use) or ‘throne/seat’ (the word’s basic sense is ‘time/occasion’ but in courtly contexts it can imply a place/seat); all are objects implied to be given.
segments.s627-g105: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). بدکنش is a compound poetic form (بد + کنش) used as a nominal: here 'ill‑doer' or 'evil actor' (the action/agent is packaged in one word). بد precedes پدر as an adjectival epithet (poetic word order; adjectives often follow the noun in modern Persian but may precede in classical verse), so بد پدر یزدگرد reads 'the bad/evil father of Yazdegerd' (پدر یزدگرد = 'Yazdegerd's father').
segments.s627-g106: به پاداش آن is a prepositional phrase 'in/for reward of that' (به + noun used adverbially). داد کردیم is a compound past tense where داد (from دادن) + کردیم (we did) together mean 'we gave' (common in older/persian poetic usage). گرد here is an adverbial/poetic particle ('around' / a rhythmic closing particle) placed after the verb phrase; it does not change the core meaning beyond emphasis/ornamentation.
segments.s627-g107: همه دل = 'every/all hearts' (همه modifies دل). ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' (ز کردار او = 'because of his conduct'). او is the third‑person pronoun 'his'. خوش کنید is the plural/honorific imperative (کنید) meaning 'make [them] happy' or 'rejoice them' — verb placed after its object phrase, typical in Persian imperative constructions.
segments.s627-g108: به آزادی = 'with/in liberty' (به used to mark manner; آزادی is used adverbially 'freely' or 'with generosity'). آهنگ here means 'tune/song' (object of the verb). آتش کنید is an imperative formed from the light verb کردن with آتش as its object: 'set (it) on fire' / 'kindle' — together 'kindle the song' i.e. 'raise a fiery song' or 'sing passionately.'
segments.s627-g109: ببخشد is the third‑person present/subjunctive form of بخشیدن ('may/does forgive' in literary/optative sense), here translated as 'forgives' or 'may forgive'. مگر functions as 'unless' (classical usage). کردگارش is 'his Creator/God' with the possessive enclitic -ش ('his'); گناه is the object 'sin'. Word order is verb first then subject+object, typical in elevated Persian style.
segments.s627-g110: ز دوزخ به مینو = a from‑to construction 'from Hell to Heaven' (ز = from, به = to). نمایدش is a single verb form: نماید (3rd sg. present/subjunctive of نِماییدن/نمودن 'to show, make appear') plus the enclitic -ش 'him' → 'shows him'. راه ('way') is the thing shown; poetic word order places the verb before its object: 'he shows him the way from Hell to Heaven.'
segments.s627-g111: کسی کو is a poetic/archaic relative construction meaning 'anyone who' (کسی = someone/anyone; کو here functions as a relative 'who' instead of the more common که). جوانست = جوان + ـست (is young). شادی کنید is the plural/honorific imperative 'make joy' / 'rejoice' (کنید). The whole clause commands: 'make merry anyone who is young.'
segments.s627-g112: دل مردمان جوان = 'the hearts of young people' (دل = heart; مردمان = people; جوان qualifies مردمان). مشکنید is the negative imperative 'do not break' (the negative is formed with the m‑ prefix plus the present stem + ِید). So: 'do not break the hearts of young people.'
segments.s627-g65: This is a question phrase: 'کجا' (where) + demonstrative 'ان' (those) + the compound 'پری‌چهرگان' (fairy‑faced ones). Word order is demonstrative after the question word, then the noun phrase and its qualifier 'جهان' (of the world). 'پری‌چهرگان' is a poetic compound meaning literally 'fairy-faced'.
segments.s627-g66: 'کز ایشان' is the archaic fused relative/prepositional form meaning 'from/of whom' (here introducing a clause about those women). 'بدی' is used as a noun 'mischief/evil' (here: the harmful or provocative effect of beauty). 'شاد' (glad/merry) qualifies 'جان' (soul/heart) and 'مهان' (guests); the sequence is noun (badness) + adjective (gladdening) + object (guests' souls). In poetry word order is compact; literally 'from whom (their) badness gladdened the souls of guests.'
segments.s627-g67: 'هرانکس که' is the indefinite relative 'whoever/whosoever' + relative particle 'که'. 'رخ زیر چادر نهفت' is a compact past clause: 'رخ' (face) + 'زیر چادر' (under the veil) + 'نهفت' (hid/covered). 'نهفت' is a past/participial form meaning 'hid' or 'was hidden', used attributively to the preceding indefinite.
segments.s627-g68: 'چنان دان' is an imperative/hortative: 'consider/know thus'. The following clause 'که گشتست با خاک جفت' is introduced by 'که' (that) and uses the perfective 'گشتست' ('has become' / 'is become') + the prepositional phrase 'با خاک' (with the earth) + 'جفت' (paired). Together the idiom means 'consider him as if he were joined/paired with the earth' — i.e. buried/dead. Note the archaic auxiliary 'گشتست'.
segments.s627-g69: 'همه دست پاکی و نیکی بریم' is hortatory: 'بریم' functions as an optative/first‑person‑plural verbal form ('let us take/let us consider'). 'همه' ('all/everyone') is the subject, 'دست پاکی و نیکی' ('hands of purity and goodness') is an idiomatic expression meaning 'to be innocent/pure and good'. The clause urges assuming purity and goodness of people.
segments.s627-g70: This is the negative parallel: 'جهان را' (the world, marked by the object/ topical marker 'را') + 'به کردار بد' (because/ by bad conduct) + 'نشمریم' ('let us not consider/let us not count'). 'نشمریم' is the negative hortative/subjunctive of 'شماریدن' (to count/consider). The whole means 'let us not judge the world by its bad deeds.'
segments.s627-g71: 'به یزدان دارنده' is a prepositional/epithet phrase 'to/for Yezdān (God), the holder/possessor' (here 'دارنده' an epithet). 'کو' is an archaic interrogative/relative (appearing as 'who' in sense) introducing 'داد فر' — 'gave farr' — where 'داد' is the past verb 'gave' and 'فر' is the technical royal term 'farr' (divine royal glory). The clause praises God as giver of royal splendour.
segments.s627-g72: A simple list of the things receiving 'فر' (farr): repeated preposition 'به' (to/for) + 'تاج' (crown), 'تخت' (throne), 'نژاد' (lineage) and 'گهر' (gems/jewels). The 'و' are coordinators. Grammatically these are direct objects/recipients of the earlier verb 'داد'.
segments.s627-g81: وگر is the conditional particle 'if' that introduces a hypothetical clause. گوسفندی is an indefinite noun 'a sheep' (the -ی suffix marks indefiniteness or a single item) placed before the verb. برند is the present/optative 3rd‑person plural form of بردن ('they take' / 'be taken'), functioning here as the verb of the conditional clause, and از رمه is a source phrase ('from the flock') following the verb.
segments.s627-g82: به introduces a temporal/locative phrase meaning 'in' or 'during' (classical usage 'towards/into' → 'in/during'). تیره شب is an adjective + noun ('dark night'), and روزگار دمه is another temporal phrase where روزگار ('fate/time') qualifies دمه ('moment, breath'), so the whole string means 'in the dark night and in the fateful moment' — a poetic doubling of time and fate.
segments.s627-g83: یکی is the numeral/article 'one/a', here marking a single item. اسپ 'steed/horse' is the direct object, modified by پرمایه ('well‑bred, substantial') placed after the noun (common in poetry). تاوان is the noun 'compensation/reparation', and the verb دهم (implied in the segment) is 1st singular present/subjunctive 'I give' or 'I will give' — in classical Persian this present/subjunctive often expresses intended action.
segments.s627-g84: مبادا is a negative optative/wish 'may it not be; lest'. که introduces the subordinate clause. بر وی is a prepositional phrase 'to/on him' (وی = 'him'), and سپاسی (with the verb نهم implied) is the noun 'thanks' so سپاسی نهم = 'that I give thanks'. The construction expresses a wish that such thanking should not occur (i.e., 'God forbid that I should thank him').
segments.s627-g85: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. با دشمنم is a prepositional phrase 'with my enemy' where the enclitic -م marks 'my'. کارزاری is a deverbal/adjectival noun meaning 'campaign/martial affair' and functions as the subject of the clause, and بود is the past copula 'was', setting the scene in the past.
segments.s627-g86: وزان is a fused particle 'and from that' (و + زان), referring back to the previous event. جنگ is 'war' and functions as the source; خسته سواری is a noun phrase 'a weary rider/horseman' (adjective before noun), and بود again is the past copula 'there was' — together meaning 'from that war there was a weary horseman.'
segments.s627-g87: فرستمش is the verb with enclitic object 'I send/send him' (classical present/subjunctive or simple 'I send/will send'); یکساله following the verb indicates duration or measure ('one‑year' / 'for a year') and modifies the monetary objects that follow. زر و درم are the coordinated objects 'gold and dirhams' (i.e., money sent). The phrase thus conveys sending a year's worth (or a year's pay) in gold and dirhams.
segments.s627-g88: نداریم is the negative present 'we/ I do not have' (poetic usage may render 1st‑person sense). فرزند او را is the object phrase 'his child' marked by را (definite object marker). دژم is a predicate adjective 'sullen/grim' — the clause is elliptical/poetic: literally 'I do not have his child; (I) am sullen,' indicating lack of offspring and a sullen disposition toward the matter.
segments.s627-g97: نباید is an impersonal negative modal meaning “one should not / it is not proper to.” ز بن is a prepositional phrase literally “from the root” or “at the root/foundation,” and کشت here is a verbal noun meaning “slaughtering/killing” (i.e. utter destruction). گاو is the object “cow/cattle.” زهی is an exclamatory interjection (vocative/exclamation) that comments on what follows. Together the segment expresses a prohibition against destroying the animal at its root.
segments.s627-g98: که introduces a relative/conditional clause “which/that.” از مرز بیرون شود is a subordinate clause where از مرز (from the border) + بیرون (out) and شود is the present subjunctive/optative “become / go,” expressing a possible condition. فرهی is a predicate adjective “noble / distinguished.” The whole clause qualifies the cow mentioned before: “which, if it goes out beyond the border, becomes noble.”
segments.s627-g99: همه رای = “all counsel/every decision.” با + مرد دانا is a prepositional phrase “with a wise man” (مرد دانا = a wise person). زنید is a 2nd-person-plural imperative from زدن used idiomatically here to mean “make/decide/settle” (i.e. take or adopt counsel). The line instructs that all decisions should be made in the company of a wise man.
segments.s627-g100: دل کودک بی‌پدر is the object phrase “the heart of a fatherless child” (دل = heart; کودک = child; بی‌پدر = fatherless/orphan). مشکنید is the negative imperative plural (“do not break”). The clause is a direct admonition: do not break the orphan’s heart.
segments.s627-g101: از اندیشه دیو باشید دور is a locative idiom: از + noun = “away from,” اندیشه دیو = “the thought of the demon” (a metaphor for evil or base thoughts), باشید دور is the polite/imperative plural “be distant / keep away.” The order places the thing to avoid first and finishes with the predicate دور “far.”
segments.s627-g102: گه is an adverb meaning “sometimes / at times.” جنگ دشمن is the noun phrase “the enemy’s war.” مجویید is the negative present-imperative/optative plural “do not seek” (مجوی + ید), with the object placed before the verb. سور here means “feast/banquet.” The line counsels occasionally refraining from seeking the enemy’s battle or pleasures (feasts).
segments.s627-g103: اگر introduces a conditional clause “if.” خواهم is 1st-person present/future of خواستن used here in a conditional sense “if I were to wish / if I wanted.” از زیردستان خراج is the object phrase “tribute/from the subjects” (از + زیردستان = from subordinates; خراج = land-tax/tribute). Together this gives “If I wished to take tribute from my subjects.”
segments.s627-g104: ز دارنده بیزارم is “I am averse/loath to the possessor” with ز (from/of) indicating the source/target of aversion and بیزارم a 1st-person present predicate “I loathe / I am averse.” و تخت عاج appends another object “and the ivory throne” (تخت عاج = ivory throne). The speaker lists things he would despise even if he took tribute.
segments.s627-g89: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/through' used to introduce the object of praise. دادار دارنده are two coordinated epithets ('Creator' and 'Possessor/holder') describing the divine addressee; Persian allows stacked nouns like this before the noun of praise. یکسر is an adverb/qualifier placed before سپاس to mean 'whole/entire/complete praise.'
segments.s627-g90: که introduces a relative/descriptive clause modifying the praised One ('who...'). اویست is a poetic contraction of او + است (= 'he is') linking the subject to the predicate that follows. جاوید نیکی‌شناس are two predicative adjectives/nouns ('eternal' + 'recognizer of good' / 'gracious, grateful knower of good') describing that subject.
segments.s627-g91: به ... به introduces two prepositional phrases 'to/toward water and to/toward fire' (repeated preposition marks each object). میازید is a present-tense, second‑person plural/formal verb; here it occurs with دست ('hand') and means a bold action or daring (literally 'you put/venture [your] hand' or idiomatically 'you dare/boast of doing so'). Word order verb-final is typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s627-g92: مگر functions as an exception marker ('except'). The following noun phrase 'هیربد مرد آتش‌پرست' names the exceptional person: هیربد = magus/chief-priest, مرد = 'man' (appositive/epithet), آتش‌پرست = 'fire‑worshipper' describing that man. The clause means 'except the magus, the fire‑worshipper.'
segments.s627-g93: مریزید is a negative imperative (do not) in poetic register used with the verb 'to shed' (blood). هم‌خون گاوان is a genitive/attributive phrase: هم‌خون ('blood‑related/kinsman') modifies گاوان ('cattle'), i.e. 'the kinsmen/related of the herd' or 'kins among the cattle.' ورز here follows as a verbal/nominal element meaning 'practice' / 'conduct' and reads as part of the admonition (an imperative tone 'do not...').
segments.s627-g94: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'). ننگست is an impersonal/predicative form 'it is shameful' (ننگ + است). در گاو کشتن is a noun phrase where در = 'in' / 'regarding', گاو کشتن = 'killing of cows' (کشتن as verbal noun). به مرز = 'at the border/frontier' gives the location; together: 'for it is shameful to kill cows at the frontier.'
segments.s627-g95: ز پیری is a causal prepositional phrase 'because of old age' (ز used poetically). مگر here has a contrastive/qualifying sense ('but/rather' in older usage) introducing the following result clause. گاو بیکار شد is a simple past/resultative clause: گاو 'the cow' + بیکار 'idle/useless' + شد 'became.'
segments.s627-g96: به چشم خداوند is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'in the eye/sight of the Lord' (به + noun phrase). خود is emphatic 'himself' / 'own' emphasizing the subject (the Lord himself). خوار شد is a predicative perfective/result 'became despised/lowly' (خوار = despised, شد = became), completing the result: 'in God's sight it became despised.'
segments.s627-g113: This clause lists two prepositional/temporal phrases ('به پیری', 'به مستی') followed by the present-tense verb میازید (‘vaunt/boast’) and the vocative/noun دست. In classical Persian poetry the verb میازید is 3rd-person present but here functions as an admonitory description addressed to ‘دست’ (vocative: “O hand, you boast…”). Word order is emphatic: the two 'به + noun' phrases front the clause to mark the grounds of the boasting.
segments.s627-g114: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('because/for'), followed by an adverb (همواره 'always') and the predicate رسوا بود. بود is the past copula here ('was'), and پیر مست is a postnominal descriptive noun phrase 'old drunk' that functions as the subject/predicate complement — i.e. 'for the old drunk was always disgraced.'
segments.s627-g115: This is an optative/negative admonition: مباشید is the negative present/subjunctive 'do not be' (polite/poetic plural or optative), with گنهکار 'sinner' as its complement and یزدان 'God' marking the object/relatum. هیـچ ('at all/never') intensifies the prohibition: 'never be a sinner toward God.'
segments.s627-g116: This clause gives a general truth: به پیری 'in old age' + به آید 'comes to' (آید = 'comes, befalls') + به رفتن 'to going/dying' with بسیچ 'much/many' qualifying it. The unusual prepositional stacking (به پیری به آید به رفتن) is poetic: 'much in the way of departing befalls in old age' (i.e. many deaths occur in old age).
segments.s627-g117: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. خشنود گردد is a present/subjunctive or potential passive-like construction meaning 'becomes pleased' (گردد = 'become'). ز ما is the poetic marker 'from/of us' (here: 'with us' or 'concerning us'), and کردگار is the subject 'the Creator/God.'
segments.s627-g118: This is a prohibitive/directional admonition: به هستی lit. 'to/for being/existence' + غم روز فردا 'the sorrow of the day to come' + مدار. In poetic usage مدار functions as a negative/limiting injunction here (roughly 'do not bring/harbour'); the clause means 'do not let the sorrow of tomorrow enter into your being' or more simply 'do not grieve for the morrow.'
segments.s627-g119: This is an optative wish: شاد باد 'may ... be happy' uses باد as the optative particle. Subject: دل زیردستان 'the hearts of the subjects/vassals'; به ما 'because of us' or 'toward us' explains the cause. Word order places the wished-for complement before باد for emphasis.
segments.s627-g120: Parallel optative: سر ... آزاد باد 'may the heads ... be free.' سر is 'head' used metaphorically (leaders/heads), سرکشان (here 'the rebellious/proud') is the group modified; از غم 'from sorrow' marks what they are freed of, and آزاد 'free' is the predicate with باد as the optative particle. The phrase thus means 'may the proud/rebellious be freed from sorrow.'
segments.s627-g33: مدارید is a hortative/imperative form (2pl) meaning 'be lenient/indulgent' and opens an address to the audience; کردار او is the noun phrase 'his conduct' (کردار = deed, او = his) placed after the verb in this poetic order; بس شگفت is a predicative phrase ('very/wondrous') with بس as an emphatic adverb and شگفت as the adjective describing کردار.
segments.s627-g34: که introduces the explanatory/result clause ('for/that'); روشن دلش is adjective + noun + enclitic pronoun (روشن = enlightened/bright, دل = heart, ش = his) and follows a poetic adjective-before-noun order; رنگ آتش گرفت is a compound predicate where رنگ (appearance/colour) is the thing 'taken' and گرفت is the perfective verb 'took' (i.e. 'took on the color of fire').
segments.s627-g35: ببینید is the imperative (2pl) 'see/observe'; تا here introduces the content of that command ('that/so that' — 'observe what...'); جم و کاوس شاه are the names/titles being pointed to (proper name جم, and کاوس with the title شاه).
segments.s627-g36: چه کردند is the main clause 'what did (they) do' (چه = what; کردند = past 3pl of کردن); کز introduces a subordinate/relative idea here with the sense 'from/through' (کز ... جستند راه = 'from the demon they sought a way'); دیو جستند راه is the subordinate clause (دیو = demon; جستند = past 3pl of جستن 'sought'; راه = way/path).
segments.s627-g37: پدر is the subject 'the father'; همچنان is an adverb 'still/likewise' indicating continuation; راه ایشان is the object/genitive 'their path' (ایشان = their); بجست is the past (3rd sg) form meaning 'he pursued/followed/sought' — together: 'the father continued to follow their way.'
segments.s627-g38: به introduces the prepositional phrase 'into/toward'; آب خرد is a genitive/compound 'water of wisdom' (آب = water, خرد = wisdom); جان تیره نشست is the main result clause where جان ('life/soul') is the subject, تیره ('dark') modifies it, and نشست (past 'sank/settled') is the verb — literally 'into the water of wisdom his life sank, darkened.'
segments.s627-g39: همه is the universal quantifier 'all/everyone'; زیردستانش is the plural possessive noun 'his subordinates' (زیردستان + ش); پیچان شدند is a predicate where پیچان (twisting/disturbed) with شدند ('became' past 3pl) describes their state ('were thrown into confusion/turning').
segments.s627-g40: فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'many/much'; ز is the classical/prepositional 'from/because of'; تندیش is a noun-form built on تند ('fierce') here meaning his fierceness/vehemence; بیجان شدند is 'became lifeless' (بیجان = lifeless, شدند = became, past 3pl), so 'many became lifeless from his ferocity.'
segments.s627-g9: پر از راستی is an adjectival phrase 'full of uprightness/truth' (پر + از + noun). کرد is the 3rd-person past of کردن and here means 'made/caused' — the verb that takes 'یکسر جهان' as its object. یکسر is an adverbial/quantifying word 'entirely/throughout' placed before جهان (poetic inversion) to yield 'the whole world'. The subject (he) is omitted as typical in narrative Persian.
segments.s627-g10: وزو is a compressed/poetic particle meaning 'from him/through him' (temporal/causal link to the prior clause). شادمانه is adverbial 'joyfully' modifying the ensuing state (copula/state is omitted). کهان و مهان are coordinated nouns 'realms/worlds and guests' (و connects them); the verb 'were joyful' is implied rather than explicit.
segments.s627-g11: هرانکس که is the indefinite relative 'whoever/whosoever'. بیداد بد is a noun + adjective 'wicked/evil oppression' (بد qualifies بیداد). دور کرد is a verbal construction where دور (away/removed) combines with the past of کردن to mean 'banished/drove away'; کرد is 3rd-person past functioning as the main verb.
segments.s627-g12: به introduces the means/reason here ('by/through'); نادادن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'not giving/withholding' used nominally. چیز and گفتار are coordinated objects ('goods/things' and 'speech'), and سرد postmodifies گفتار as 'stern/aloof' — 'by withholding things and (by) stern speech.'
segments.s627-g13: وزان پس ('after that, then') is a temporal connective ('وزان' = from that time; پس = then). چنین گفت is 'spoke thus' where گفت is the past 3rd-person of گفتن ('he said/spoke') and با موبدان is a prepositional phrase 'with the mobeds' indicating interlocutors.
segments.s627-g14: که‌ای here leads into a direct address; read as a vocative ('O ...'). پرهنر and پاک‌دل are descriptive adjectives 'learned/cultured' and 'pure‑hearted' placed before the addressee noun. بخردان is a vocative plural 'wise ones' (ب- prefix + خرد root), so the whole is an archaic/vocative formula 'O learned, pure‑hearted wise men.'
segments.s627-g15: جهان را uses the object/topic marker را ('the world' as definite object). ز هرگونه is an adverbial phrase 'in every manner/from every kind' (ز = in/from; هرگونه = in any way). دارید is 2nd-person plural present 'you have/hold' and یاد is a noun 'memory/remembrance' functioning as the object complement: 'hold the world's memory in every way' = 'keep the world in mind in every respect.'
segments.s627-g16: ز کردار شاهان is a prepositional source phrase 'from the deeds of kings' (ز = from/of; کردار = deeds; شاهان = kings). بیداد و داد are two coordinated nouns 'injustice and justice' — the pair specifies the kinds of deeds to be remembered. Word order is source + noun + coordinated qualities, typical in epic diction.
segments.s627-g17: بسی (prepositive quantifier) modifies دستِ شاهان: classical word order has the modifier before the noun phrase. دستِ شاهان is a genitive construction ('the hand/power of the kings'). ز introduces the thing from/of which the hands are emptied (here linking to the verb تهی ماند in the next segment). In this clause بیداد و آز are the coordinated nouns giving the content 'injustice and greed.'
segments.s627-g18: تهی ماند is a two-word predicate: تهی (devoid/of which something is empty) + ماند (past 'remained/was left'). The verb ماند is past tense here and applies to the preceding nominal phrase; the conjunction و plus هم begins a parallel clause. هم marks the second noun (تن) as 'also/likewise.' ز again introduces what the body is empty of (آرام و ناز). The second clause omits a repeated copula (i.e., 'was left' is understood).
segments.s627-g19: The clause has subject جهان followed by a prepositional/causal phrase از بداندیش and the locative phrase در بیم plus the past copula بود. از here indicates the source/cause ('from/of the ill‑minded'), در بیم = 'in fear,' and بود is the past tense 'was.'
segments.s627-g20: دلِ نیک‌مردان is a possessive/genitive noun phrase ('the hearts of the virtuous men'). به دو نیم is a directional/figurative phrase meaning 'into two halves' (به here = 'into'), and بود is the past copula 'were/was' linking the state (split) to the subject.
segments.s627-g21: "همه" is the universal subject 'everyone/all'. The phrase "دست کرده به کار بدی" is an idiom: literally 'had put (their) hand to an evil deed' meaning 'took up evil'; "دست کرده" uses the perfect/participial form کرده (literally 'done/put') with the action-oriented noun دست ('hand') plus the preposition به introducing the object کار ('work/deed') modified by بدی ('evil'). Word order here is topical subject + verbal participle + prepositional object (typical of Persian poetic economy).
segments.s627-g22: نبد is an archaic/poetic negative existential ('there was not' / 'did not exist'), placed before the phrase it negates. "کسی را" here marks the beneficiary/indirect object ('for anyone') with the object/topic marker را after کسی; "کوشش ایزدی" is a noun phrase 'divine effort/help' (کوشش 'effort' modified by the adjective/attributive ایزدی 'divine'). Together: 'there was no divine effort for anyone.'
segments.s627-g23: Again نبد is the negative existential 'there was not'. It introduces the locative/prepositional phrase "بر زن و زاده" ('over/on women and offspring') where بر = 'over/on' and زن و زاده lists the affected groups. کس here emphasizes the absence ('no one') and پادشا ('king') is the predicate noun; literal order places the verb first (poetic inversion): 'There was no one king over women and children.'
segments.s627-g24: "پر از" is a fixed adjective+preposition pattern meaning 'full of'. "غم دل" is a genitive/compound 'sorrow of heart' (i.e., 'heart-sorrow'), and "مردم پارسا" is 'the pious people' (noun followed by its adjective). The whole phrase reads '(was) full of sorrow (was) the heart of the pious people' — in English: 'the hearts of the pious people were full of sorrow.'
segments.s627-g73: که‌گر is an archaic/poetic conditional particle meaning "if." کارداری is a noun meaning someone experienced or crafty (the subject of the condition). به یک مشک خاک is a prepositional phrase modifying the motive/measure (به = 'for/over/with', یک = 'one/a single', مشک خاک = 'musk-dust' or 'a pinch of musk'); word order is poetic (prepositional phrase after the subject).
segments.s627-g74: زیان جوید is a verbal phrase: جوید (3rd person singular present/subjunctive narrative) = 'seeks/searches for' and زیان = 'harm/loss' — together 'seeks harm' or 'tries to cause loss.' اندر is the emphatic/poetic preposition 'in/within.' بلند و مغاک lists two places (بلند = 'height/lofty place', مغاک = 'abyss'); و simply links them.
segments.s627-g75: هم‌انجا is an adverb meaning 'right there / at that very place.' بسوزم is first‑person singular subjunctive/potential of سوزدن used here with a forceful/factual force 'I will burn' (poetic speech). به آتش is the instrument/locative 'in/by fire.' تنش ('تن' + enclitic ش) is the object 'his body.'
segments.s627-g76: کنم is 1st‑person singular of کردن used here as 'I will make / cause.' بر سر دار is a locative phrase 'on the head/top of the gallows' (سر = 'top/head', دار = 'gallows' in this context). پیراهنش = 'his shirt' (پیراهن + enclitic ش); overall the verb + phrase mean 'I will put/make his shirt upon the gallows.'
segments.s627-g77: وگر is an alternative conditional 'if / but if.' در گذشته ز شب is a temporal phrase 'during/through the course of the night that passed' (گذشته used poetically for 'passed' or 'elapsed'). چند پاس = 'a few watches/rounds' (پاس = sentry/watch), indicating portions of the night.
segments.s627-g78: بدزدد is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of دزدیدن 'to steal' used here narratively: 'he steals.' ز = 'from.' درویش is the owner 'the dervish/poor man' (possessive role). دزدی پلاس is the object: دزدی (the act/noun 'theft') qualifying پلاس ('coarse blanket'); word order places the verbal noun before its object in a compact poetic phrase—meaning 'steals the coarse blanket from the dervish.'
segments.s627-g79: به تاوانش means 'as his recompense/for his punishment' (تاوان + enclitic ش). دیبا is the direct object 'brocade.' فرستم is 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of فرستادن used in a resolute/future sense 'I will send.' ز گنج = 'from the treasury/treasure' (ز = poetic 'from').
segments.s627-g80: بشویم here functions as a verbal intent 'I will cleanse/relieve' (poetic subjunctive); the exact root sense 'to wash/cleanse' is being used, but that sense is not present in the provided sense list. دل غمگنان را is the object: دل = 'heart', غمگنان = 'the sorrowful' (adjective/noun), را the definite object marker. ز رنج = 'from suffering' (ز = 'from', رنج = 'suffering'). Together: 'I will wash/cleanse the hearts of the sorrowful from suffering.'
segments.s627-g41: کنون (now) sets the temporal frame; رفت is the past/perfective verb 'he went/has gone' describing his departure. ز + او (written here as زو) means 'of/from him' and links to the following noun phrase; نام بد 'a bad name' = 'ill reputation' is the patient left behind, with ماند 'remained/is left' as the verb. The final و بس is a short appended comment meaning 'and that's all' / 'only that remains.'
segments.s627-g42: همی here is an emphatic/continuous particle ('still/indeed') introducing the clause; آفرین is a noun 'praise/acclamation' and نیابد is the negative present/classical form 'does not obtain/find' (ن‑ + یابد). ز کس = 'from anyone' (with the negative verb giving the sense 'from no one'). The clause therefore states that he obtains no praise from anyone.
segments.s627-g43: ز ما باد is an optative formula: 'may there be from us...' (باد = optative 'may it be'). بر جان او 'upon his soul' is the target; آفرین is the noun 'praise' so the literal order is 'from us may be upon his soul praise' → 'may our praise rest upon his soul.'
segments.s627-g44: مبادا introduces a negative wish 'may it not be / lest'; که introduces the subordinate clause. پیچد is a present/optative 3rd-person form 'may it twist/turn' and روانش = روان + ـش 'his spirit/soul'. ز کین = 'from/with hatred/vengeance' shows cause. The whole clause prays that his spirit not be twisted by vengeance.
segments.s627-g45: کنون 'now' opens the sentence. بر + نشستم here means 'I took my place/ I sat down (before/upon)' — a classical use meaning 'I sat at/attended.' بر گاه اوی = 'at his court/seat' (گاه in the sense of 'place/occasion' and اوی is the archaic/genitive 'his'). Note: the printed senses for نشستم do not reflect its verbal sense 'I sat', so the sense index is not available.
segments.s627-g46: به مینو کشد راه اوی is literal 'his path draws to heaven' — idiomatically 'his way leads to heaven.' به مینو 'toward heaven', بی‌گمان 'undoubtedly' is an adverbial, راه اوی 'his path.' کشد is the 3rd-person singular of کشیدن 'to draw/lead' in this sense. (The sense list given for کشد does not include this specific sense, so its index is not available.)
segments.s627-g47: همی خواهم is a classical construction meaning 'I continually/willingly ask/ desire'; از کردگار جهان = 'from the Maker of the world' is the prepositional object of خواهم. The sequence is a polite petition to God.
segments.s627-g48: که introduces the content of the request: نیرو دهد = 'that He give power/strength' (دهد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive here as the verb of the subordinate wish). آشکار و نهان 'manifest and hidden' (openly and secretly) are coordinated adverbs/adjectival complements specifying the scope of the requested power.
segments.s627-g49: که here introduces a subordinate content clause ('that' / 'so that') and links this clause to the wish or exhortation of the surrounding lines. با زیردستان is a prepositional phrase (با = 'with') where زیردستان (subjects/subordinates) is the object of the preposition. مدارا کنیم is a hortative/subjunctive 1st-person-plural verb phrase: کنیم ('we do') with the noun مدارا ('tolerance, forbearance') functioning as the verbal object or complement — literally 'that we do tolerance with (toward) our subordinates' = 'that we be tolerant with our subordinates.'
segments.s627-g50: ز is the poetic form of از ('from'), introducing the source. خاک سیه is a noun + adjective phrase with the adjective following the noun (typical poetic word order): 'black earth' or 'black dust.' مشک is the noun 'musk' which is the thing being produced; سارا here occurs immediately after مشک and functions as a modifier attached to the musk (in the printed data it is given as a proper name), and کن̣یم is again the hortative/subjunctive 'we make' (کنیم) — the whole phrase meaning roughly 'and from black earth we make musk.'
segments.s627-g51: که again introduces the clause. با خاک is a prepositional phrase 'with/by the earth.' چون here functions as a temporal/comparative conjunction 'when/as' introducing the subordinate temporal clause: چون جفت گردد = 'when it becomes paired/joined.' جفت ('pair' / 'joined') is used nominally; گردد is the classical/poetic 3rd-person singular of 'become' (گردیدن/گشتن). تنم is 'my body' in usual syntax, placed after the verb phrase for poetic inversion: literally 'when (my) body becomes paired with the earth.'
segments.s627-g52: نگیرد is the negative present/subjunctive/optative 3rd-person form of گرفتن ('to take, seize') and here means 'will not take' / 'does not seize.' ستمدیده‌ای is an indefinite noun with the -ای suffix meaning 'an oppressed person' (one who is afflicted), serving as an agent in this clause. دامنم ('my hem/skirts') is the object (possessive -م), placed after the verb for emphasis in poetic word order. The sense: 'an oppressed one will not seize my skirt.'
segments.s627-g53: شما is the explicit 2nd-person plural/formal subject ('you'). همچنین is an adverb meaning 'likewise' or 'similarly' modifying the command. چادر راستی is a noun phrase 'the cloak/veil (چادر) of righteousness/uprightness' where راستی ('uprightness, truth') qualifies چادر; the adjective follows in this poetic order. This segment sets up an imperative addressed to 'you.'
segments.s627-g54: بپوشید is the 2nd-person-plural imperative/hortative of پوشیدن ('put on; wear; cover'), commanding the addressees to 'put on' the preceding cloak. شسته دل is an adjectival phrase 'washed heart' (شسته = 'washed/cleaned' modifying دل = 'heart'), a common idiom meaning 'pure/cleansed of corruption.' از indicates separation/source ('of/from') and کاستی ('deficiency/shortcoming') is the noun governed by از: together 'with a heart washed of shortcomings' or 'having a heart cleansed of defect.' The phrase follows the verb in inverted, poetic order.
segments.s627-g55: که again introduces the clause. جز is the exception marker 'except.' مرگ is 'death,' and را is the definite-object/ topicalizing marker attached to مرگ to mark it as the exception. کس is 'anyone/person' (used here in a general negative or universal sense). ز مادر نزاد is an older/poetic verb phrase: ز (poetic از = 'from') + مادر ('mother') + نزاد (classical form related to زادن/زاییدن 'to beget / to be born') — literally 'was born from a mother.' The whole clause says 'that, aside from Death, no one was born of a mother.'
segments.s627-g56: ز introduces the source/lineage 'from' and is followed by a coordinated list of peoples (دهقان, تازی, رومی). In classical/persian poetic listing, the head noun نژاد ('race/lineage') comes after the list, giving 'of the descent/lineage of the dehqān, the Tazi (Arabs), and the Rûmî (Romans/Byzantines).' The structure places the enumerated origins before the summarizing noun نژاد.
segments.s627-g25: به هر جای introduces a locative: 'to/at every place' (به + هر + جای). گستردن is the infinitive/present-nominal form 'to spread/extend' that governs دست دیو ('the demon's hand'); the whole phrase reads 'wherever the demon stretched out his hand'. Word order is poetic: preposition + determiner + place + verb-noun + object.
segments.s627-g26: بریده is a past-participle/adjectival form 'torn/cut' modifying دل 'heart'. از marks cause ('from/because of'), so از بیم گیهان = 'from/because of fear for the world'. خدیو is the affected person (the ruler) whose heart is described. The clause therefore: 'the khedive's heart was torn because of fear for the world.'
segments.s627-g27: سر نیکویها = 'the heads (or leaders/mental dispositions) of the good' (سر used metaphorically/abstractly). و connects the clauses: دست بدیست = 'but the hand is bad' (بدیست = 'it is bad/evil'). The clause contrasts good heads with bad hands; دست here implies action/power rather than mere anatomy.
segments.s627-g28: در introduces a locative/field: 'in knowledge and effort'. بخردیست is a predicative older form meaning 'there is wisdom / it is wise' (ب + خرد + است). So the whole clause states the location/source of wisdom: 'wisdom is in learning and effort.'
segments.s627-g29: همه پاک ('all entirely/wholly') is the subject phrase; در گردن پادشاست is an idiomatic locative: 'is on the king's neck' meaning 'lies with the king / is the king's responsibility'. پادشاست is an archaic/poetic form of 'پادشاه است'.
segments.s627-g30: که introduces a result/relative clause 'so/that'. پیدا شود = 'become visible/apparent' (پیدا used adjectivally with شود). ز + و (زونده orthography for از او) = 'from him' (ز = poetic از, و here represents the clitic pronoun 'او'). همه کژ و راست = 'all crooked and straight' i.e. 'all wrong and right'. The clause: 'so that from him all wrong and right are revealed.'
segments.s627-g31: پدر‌گر is the conditional compound (پدر + گر) meaning 'if the father' (گر = if). به بیداد یازید دست is a verb phrase: یازید (from an archaic root) here means 'turned to / reached out' or 'resorted to', with به بیداد 'to injustice' as its object. دست is the noun that will be negated in the next clause (i.e. 'there remained no hand...').
segments.s627-g32: نبد is the archaic negative copula 'نبود' = 'there was not / there remained not'. It negates the following predicate phrase پاک و دانا و یزدان‌پرست = 'pure, wise, and God‑worshipping (pious)'. Grammatically, this says that if the father/ruler committed injustice, then no hand remained that could be called pure/wise/pious.
segments.s627-g57: به کردار شیرست is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in manner/behaviour (he is) lion-like.' The copula 'است' is omitted (typical in classical Persian). آهنگ اوی is a possessed noun phrase: آهنگ 'pace/bearing' + اوی (archaic possessive 'his'), so together: 'his bearing is lion-like.'
segments.s627-g58: نپیچد is negative present/subjunctive form of پیچید, here used as a general negative assertion ('does not/cannot twist'). کسی with the negation yields 'no one.' گردن از چنگ اوی is a prepositional/object phrase 'a neck from his claw' (از = 'from/out of', اوی = 'his'), so the clause says no one can twist a neck out of his grip.
segments.s627-g59: همان شیر درنده را is a focused definite object ('that very/that ferocious lion' + object marker را). بشکرد is an archaic past verb meaning 'shattered/broke' (third person), so 'he shattered that ferocious lion.'
segments.s627-g60: به خواری تن اژدها بسپرد is a second verb clause with the same subject: به خواری ('into humiliation') + تن اژدها ('the body of the dragon') + بسپرد (past of سپردن 'to hand over/consign'). Literally: 'he consigned the dragon's body to humiliation.'
segments.s627-g61: کجا here is an exclamatory/interrogative 'where (is)...' The verb ('are') is omitted, so آن سر و تاج شاهنشهان is the verbless noun phrase 'that head and crown of the emperors.' The phrase laments their absence.
segments.s627-g62: Again کجا introduces a lament. آن بزرگان و فرخ مهان is a coordinated noun phrase: بزرگان 'the great ones' and فرخ مهان 'fortunate/illustrious nobles/guests.' The clause omits a copula and asks 'where are they?'
segments.s627-g63: کجا آن سواران گردنکشان is a rhetorical question 'Where are those horsemen...' گردنکشان is a descriptive plural noun meaning 'neck-throwers' = proud/arrogant men (an epithet), modifying سواران 'riders/horsemen.'
segments.s627-g64: کزیشان is a relative/compound pronoun ('of whom' / 'from whom') referring back to the horsemen. نبینم is first-person negative present ('I do not see'); به گیتی 'in the world' and نشان 'a sign/trace.' Together: 'of whom I see no trace in the world.'
segments.s627-g121: همه نامداران is the subject phrase 'all the renowned (men)'. چو here is a conjunction meaning 'when' that introduces the subordinate time clause. گفتار شاه is a genitive noun phrase 'the king's speech' (گفتار = 'speech', شاه = 'king').
segments.s627-g122: شنیدند is the simple past plural 'they heard'. و connects the clauses. کردند نیکو نگاه is a poetic inversion of نگاه کردند نیکو: here کردن + noun (نگاه) forms a verbal idiom 'to cast/regard (a look)'; نیکو qualifies that look as 'good/kind', so the whole means 'they regarded him kindly' or 'gave him a favorable look'.
segments.s627-g123: همه is 'all/everyone'. دیده کردند is literally 'they made (their) eyes' — an idiom meaning 'they looked' or 'they turned their eyes'. پیشش means 'before him / in his presence'. پر آب is a postpositive adjective phrase 'full of water' (i.e., teary-eyed); پر modifies آب, forming the image 'eyes full of water'.
segments.s627-g124: ازان شاه is a demonstrative phrase 'that king' (ازان = 'from that/that'), with the copula omitted in classical Persian. پردانش و زودیاب are predicate adjectives describing him: پردانش 'learned/knowledgeable' and زودیاب an adjective meaning 'quick-finding' or 'quick to find/perceive'.
segments.s627-g125: خروشان is an adverbial/participle 'roaring(ly)' describing how they spoke. برو here functions as an exclamatory/intensifying particle placed before the interjection (an older/classical usage rather than the literal imperative 'go'). آفرین is the exclamation 'bravo' or 'well done'. خواندند is the past plural 'they uttered/called out' (here 'they cried out').
segments.s627-g126: ورا is an object pronoun/adverb meaning 'to him' or 'on him'. پادشا زمین is a title phrase 'king of the land' (پادشا = 'king', زمین = 'land/country'). خواندند again is the past plural verb 'they called/proclaimed', so the clause means 'they proclaimed him king of the land.'

sentences.s627-s1: When the king had finished the Roman affair, his heart became twisted (anxious) over the business of the army.
sentences.s627-s2: He ordered that a mobed be made a counselor, to sit in council with a certain renowned man.
sentences.s627-s3: He bestowed (gave) throughout the face of the earth the foremost, quarrelsome champions.
sentences.s627-s4: He gave dirhams and steeds and gems and a hat, and (bestowed) the precious realm, crown, and throne/seat.
sentences.s627-s53: If the ill‑doer was Yazdegerd's bad father, we gave a reward for that.
sentences.s627-s54: Make every heart glad because of his deeds; freely raise a fiery song.
sentences.s627-s55: Unless his Creator forgives his sin, He will show him the way from Hell to Heaven.
sentences.s627-s56: Rejoice anyone who is young; do not break the hearts of young people.
sentences.s627-s33: Where are those fairy-faced ones of the world whose mischief (or charm) made the guests' souls rejoice?
sentences.s627-s34: Whoever hid his face beneath the veil, consider him as if he had been paired with the earth (i.e. dead).
sentences.s627-s35: Let us regard everyone as having pure hands and goodness; let us not judge the world by bad deeds.
sentences.s627-s36: To God, the Holder — who gave the farr (divine glory) to crown and to throne and to lineage and to gems.
sentences.s627-s41: And if a sheep is taken from the flock in the dark of night and in a fateful moment,
sentences.s627-s42: I will give one fine steed as compensation; may it not be that I give him thanks.
sentences.s627-s43: When there was a campaign against my enemy, from that war there came a weary horseman.
sentences.s627-s44: I shall (or I did) send him a year's worth of gold and dirhams; I have no child of his — I am sullen.
sentences.s627-s49: One should not utterly slay a cow at its root; ah — the one that goes beyond the frontier may become noble.
sentences.s627-s50: Take all counsel with a wise man; do not break the heart of a fatherless child.
sentences.s627-s51: Keep yourself far from demonic thoughts; at times do not seek the enemy’s war or the feast.
sentences.s627-s52: If I were to exact tribute from my subjects, I would be averse to the possessor and to an ivory throne.
sentences.s627-s45: All praise to the Creator and Possessor, for he is eternal and a knower of good.
sentences.s627-s46: You put your hand to water and to fire (you dare both), except the magus, the fire‑worshipping man.
sentences.s627-s47: Do not spill the blood of (kinsmen among) the cattle; for it is shameful to slaughter cows at the frontier.
sentences.s627-s48: Because of old age the cow became idle, and in the sight of the Lord it became despised.
sentences.s627-s57: O hand, do not vaunt yourself on old age or on drunkenness; for the old drunk was always disgraced.
sentences.s627-s58: Be not a sinner before God at all; in old age much comes in the way of departing (i.e. many die).
sentences.s627-s59: When the Creator is pleased with us, do not carry grief for the morrow into your being.
sentences.s627-s60: May the hearts of our subjects be glad because of us; may the heads of the rebellious be free from sorrow.
sentences.s627-s17: Be indulgent toward his exceedingly strange conduct, for his enlightened heart took on the color of fire.
sentences.s627-s18: Observe what Jam and King Kavus did — how they sought a way from the demon.
sentences.s627-s19: The father likewise followed their path; into the water of wisdom his life sank, darkened.
sentences.s627-s20: All his subordinates were thrown into turmoil; many, from his fierceness, became lifeless.
sentences.s627-s5: He made the whole world entirely full of uprightness, and because of him the realms and guests rejoiced.
sentences.s627-s6: Whoever drove away wicked oppression — by withholding goods and by blunt speech.
sentences.s627-s7: After that he thus spoke with the mobeds: 'O learned, pure‑hearted, wise ones,'
sentences.s627-s8: Keep the world in mind in every way; remember the deeds of kings — both injustice and justice.
sentences.s627-s9: Many a king's hand was left empty from injustice and greed, and likewise his body from rest and indulgence.
sentences.s627-s10: The world was in fear of ill‑minded people; the hearts of the virtuous were split in two.
sentences.s627-s11: All had set their hands to evil; there was no divine help for anyone.
sentences.s627-s12: There was no king over woman or offspring; the hearts of the pious people were full of sorrow.
sentences.s627-s37: If a crafty person, for the sake of a single pinch of musk-dust, seeks to do harm in the heights and in the abyss.
sentences.s627-s38: There I will burn his body in the fire and I will make his shirt hang upon the gallows.
sentences.s627-s39: And if, during several watches of the night that passed, he steals — he steals a coarse blanket from a dervish,
sentences.s627-s40: I will send brocade from the treasury as his recompense, and I will cleanse the hearts of the sorrowful from suffering.
sentences.s627-s21: Now he has gone, and only an ill name remains of him; he finds no praise from anyone.
sentences.s627-s22: May our praise be upon his soul; may it not be that his spirit be twisted by hatred.
sentences.s627-s23: Now I sat at his court; surely his path leads to heaven.
sentences.s627-s24: I ask (continually) of the Maker of the world that He grant power, both manifest and hidden.
sentences.s627-s25: that we show forbearance toward our subordinates, and from black earth we make musk.
sentences.s627-s26: that when my body is joined to the earth, an oppressed one will not seize my skirt.
sentences.s627-s27: You likewise don the cloak of uprightness; have a heart washed free of shortcomings.
sentences.s627-s28: that, except for Death, no one was born of a mother; (we are) of the stock of dehqān, Tazi, and Rûmî.
sentences.s627-s13: Wherever the demon stretched out his hand, the khedive's heart was torn with fear for the world.
sentences.s627-s14: Their heads are good but their hands are bad; in knowledge and effort lies wisdom.
sentences.s627-s15: Everything rests entirely on the king's neck (i.e. on the king), so that from him all crooked and straight deeds become apparent.
sentences.s627-s16: If the father (ruler) stretched out his hand in injustice, there remained no hand that was pure, wise, and pious.
sentences.s627-s29: He bore himself like a lion; no one could wrench a neck from his claw.
sentences.s627-s30: He shattered that ferocious lion and consigned the dragon's body to humiliation.
sentences.s627-s31: Where is that head and crown of the emperors? Where are those great men and fortunate nobles?
sentences.s627-s32: Where are those proud horsemen? Of them I see no sign in the world.
sentences.s627-s61: When all the famed men heard the king's speech, they listened and looked upon him favorably.
sentences.s627-s62: They all turned their eyes toward him, their eyes full of water; that king was learned and quick to discover.
sentences.s627-s63: They cried out 'Bravo!' with a roar, and they proclaimed him king of the land.
